perturbation.original_text: 4.1 Confidentiality Obligation Both parties will maintain all Confidential Information in confidence with at least the same degree of care that it uses to protect its ow n confidential and propri etary information, but no less than a reasonable degree of care unde r the circumstances and will not disclose the Confidential Information except as necessary for its authorized participants to evaluate and comment thereon.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1 Confidentiality Obligation Both parties will maintain all Confidential Information in confidence and will not disclose the Confidential Information except as necessary for its authorized participants to evaluate and comment thereon.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for 'the same degree of care' significantly weakens the confidentiality obligations, potentially conflicting with California's requirements for reasonable care in protecting trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. Removing the 'same degree of care' language weakens the standard of care, potentially failing to meet the 'reasonable measures' requirement, especially if the company's normal care is higher than what is now contractually obligated.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates maintaining confidentiality with at least the same degree of care used to protect one's own confidential information, but no less than a reasonable degree of care. The changed text removes the 'same degree of care' requirement, weakening the standard. The Law Explanation suggests this could fail to meet the 'reasonable measures' requirement of CUTSA. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' which can occur when someone has a duty to maintain secrecy and then discloses or uses the trade secret without consent. By removing the higher standard of care, the changed text potentially reduces the level of protection afforded to confidential information, creating a contradiction because the agreement no longer explicitly requires the same level of protection the company uses for its own information or a reasonable degree of care.

perturbation.original_text: 5.4 Notices Any notices under this Agreement shall be sent by a) registered mail; b) tracked overnight carrier or c) email transmission where the recipient specifically replies to acknowledge receipt.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.4 Notices Any notices under this Agreement shall be sent by email transmission.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the registered mail and overnight carrier options for notices could conflict with California law regarding service of legal notices, which may require specific methods to ensure proof of delivery, especially concerning termination or breach of contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1013 specifies methods for service by mail, including requirements for proof of service. Limiting notices to email without a guaranteed receipt confirmation mechanism (beyond a simple reply) could be deemed insufficient, particularly for legal notices requiring proof of delivery under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1013 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 5. Notices, and Filing and Service of Papers [1010 - 1020] ( Chapter 5 enacted 1872. ) 1013. (a) In case of service by mail, the notice or other paper shall be deposited in a post office, mailbox, subpost office, substation, or mail chute, or other like facility regularly maintained by the United States Postal Service, in a sealed envelope, with postage paid, addressed to the person on whom it is to be served, at the office address as last given by that person on any document filed in the cause and served on the party making service by mail; otherwise at that party’s place of residence. Service is complete at the time of the deposit, but any period of notice and any right or duty to do any act or make any response within any period or on a date certain after service of the document, which time period or date is prescribed by statute or rule of court, shall be extended five calendar days, upon service by mail, if the place of address and the place of mailing is within the State of California, 10 calendar days if either the place of mailing or the place of address is outside the State o
perturbation.justification: The changed text limits acceptable methods of notice to email transmission only, removing options for registered mail and tracked overnight carrier. This contradicts California Code of Civil Procedure § 1013, which, according to Scraped Law Snippet 1, addresses service by mail. The snippet specifies the requirements for service by mail, including depositing the notice in a post office or mailbox with postage paid. While the snippet doesn't explicitly mandate mail as the *only* method of service, removing it introduces the risk that legally required notices may not be properly served, especially if proof of delivery is needed. The email-only method, without guaranteed receipt confirmation, might not meet legal standards for certain notices, creating a potential legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 5.7 No Warranty ALL PARTIES ACKNOWLEDGE THAT ALL INFORM ATION PROVIDED UNDE R THIS AGREEMENT, INCLUDING ANY SPECIFICATIONS AND CONTRIBU TIONS, ARE PROVIDED “AS IS” WITH NO WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS WHATSOEVER, WHETHER EXPRESS, IMPLIED, STATUTORY, OR OTHERWISE, AND THE PARTIES EXPRESSL Y DISCLAIM ANY WARRANTY OR CONDITION OF MERCHANTABILITY, SECURITY, SATIS FACTORY QUALITY, NONINFRINGEMENT INCLUDING NON-INFRINGEMENT OF THIRD PARTY INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS, FITNESS FOR ANY PARTICULAR PURPOSE, ERROR-FREE OPERATION, OR ANY WARRANTY OR CONDITION OTHERWISE ARISING OUT OF ANY PROPOSAL, CONTRIBUTION, SPECIFICATION, OR SAMPLE.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.7 No Warranty ALL PARTIES ACKNOWLEDGE THAT ALL INFORM ATION PROVIDED UNDE R THIS AGREEMENT, INCLUDING ANY SPECIFICATIONS AND CONTRIBU TIONS, ARE PROVIDED “AS IS” WITH NO WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS WHATSOEVER, WHETHER EXPRESS, IMPLIED, STATUTORY, OR OTHERWISE, AND THE PARTIES EXPRESSL Y DISCLAIM ANY WARRANTY OR CONDITION OF SECURITY, SATIS FACTORY QUALITY, NONINFRINGEMENT INCLUDING NON-INFRINGEMENT OF THIRD PARTY INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS, FITNESS FOR ANY PARTICULAR PURPOSE, ERROR-FREE OPERATION, OR ANY WARRANTY OR CONDITION OTHERWISE ARISING OUT OF ANY PROPOSAL, CONTRIBUTION, SPECIFICATION, OR SAMPLE.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the disclaimer of 'merchantability', the agreement could be interpreted to implicitly include a warranty of merchantability, which may not be desirable for Khronos if they are providing specifications or contributions that are experimental or under development. This could create liability under the California Commercial Code if the information is not fit for the ordinary purpose for which it's used.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 2314 implies a warranty of merchantability in contracts for the sale of goods. Removing the explicit disclaimer, and because this agreement concerns the sharing of information related to specifications, a court might interpret this as an implied warranty that the provided information is fit for its ordinary purpose, exposing Khronos to potential liability if the information is flawed or incomplete.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2314 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2314. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) Pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) In the case of fungible goods, are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) Are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) Run, within the variations permitted by the agreement, of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) Are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) Conform to the promises or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the explicit disclaimer of "merchantability". According to the Law Explanation, California Commercial Code § 2314 implies a warranty of merchantability in contracts for the sale of goods. Scraped Law Snippet 1, COM 2314 (1) states that "Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind." By removing the disclaimer, the contract now potentially implies a warranty of merchantability, contradicting the original intent to provide information "AS IS" without such warranties. This creates a legal contradiction because the omission leads to the possible implication of a warranty that was explicitly disclaimed in the original contract.

perturbation.original_text: 5.5 Governing Law This Agreement shall be governed by and inte rpreted in accordance with the laws of the State of California, excluding its choice of laws rules. The parties hereby agree that any dispute regarding the interpretation or valid ity of, or otherwise arising out of, this Agreement shall be subject to the exclusive ju risdiction of the California state courts of Santa Clara County, California (or, if there is fe deral jurisdiction, the Un ited States District Court for the Northern District of California, San Jose), and the parties agree to submit to the personal and exclus ive jurisdiction and ve nue of these courts.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.5 Governing Law This Agreement shall be governed by and inte rpreted in accordance with the laws of a jurisdiction of Khronos' choosing. The parties hereby agree that any dispute regarding the interpretation or valid ity of, or otherwise arising out of, this Agreement shall be subject to the jurisdiction of a court of Khronos' choosing.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law and jurisdiction to a location of Khronos' choosing removes the Participant's right to have the agreement interpreted under California law and to have disputes resolved in California courts. If the Participant is a California-based entity or individual, this change could be deemed unconscionable and violate California's public policy regarding fair contractual terms, particularly if the chosen jurisdiction has laws less favorable to the Participant.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1670.5 allows a court to refuse to enforce a contract or clause found to be unconscionable at the time it was made. Forcing a California party to litigate in a distant jurisdiction with unfamiliar laws could be deemed procedurally and substantively unconscionable, especially if there's a significant power imbalance between the parties. This change could violate California's public policy against unconscionable contracts.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1670.5 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1670.5. (a) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (b) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose, and effect to aid the court in making the determination. (Added by Stats. 1979, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Civil Code § 1670.5. The original text specifies California law and courts, while the changed text allows Khronos to choose the governing law and jurisdiction. According to the Law Explanation, forcing a California party to litigate in a distant jurisdiction could be deemed unconscionable, and the scraped law snippet 1 states that 'If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract'. Therefore, by removing the guarantee of California law and jurisdiction, the changed text introduces the risk of unconscionability, directly contradicting the protections provided by California Civil Code § 1670.5.
